speaker: interviewer
text: 6th of December, 1995, interview of Eva Hoffman, tape number four. You were saying that you went into the warehouses. Where were the Germans at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the Germans already capitulated. And they were going around prisoners themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had taken them prisoners? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know really what happened. But the English people came in. And they gave up to the Englander.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that-- where were you when the English people came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I presume I was in the barrack 208 where I actually lived, should we say? And we went. And everybody done whatever they could to better themselves. We even got once a bag of flour, which one we didn't know what to do with it. We sold the bag of flour to two men for cigarettes. We gave-- we gave them the cigarettes. He gave us the bag of flour-- like exchange. I mean, this was doing business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what did we do with the flour? I don't remember. But we did have a bag of flour. And after that, the-- the Germans were taken away, I presume. The English people, with the soldiers, they were very nice and kind to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only big mistake what they made-- the seeing those starved people, they gave us K-rations. K-rations were like cold beans. It was good for starved people with pieces of beef in it and alike-- and powdered milk. And they gave us cocoa, and cookies, and cigarettes in those K-rations. People were going crazy. They were eating themselves stupid. And that's how we lost a lot of people account of the food just didn't agree with them. I was in a very bad stage too. The food went through me-- the where I eat, the way that it went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the food didn't do me any good. I became sick from it for a long, long time. After a few-- maybe a week or so, they came. And they evacuated us to take us to-- somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: First they came around to spray us with DDT-- to de-lice us. We had plenty of that. And since I had Fleck typhus, they were very happily sticking in my sores. All-- all the lice were sticking in my sores. And naturally, that helped-- the DDT-- what they sprayed. That cleared the lice. But the sores wouldn't heal account of I had malnutrition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came, the English people, and took us away from the camp to a different camp. I don't know how far that was-- put us on like military trucks. And from the 1,000 girls we started off in the barrack 208, I presume 90-some be remained. The girls were dying like flies.

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was too good for weakened conditions. There was too-- too much nutrition or whatever-- they couldn't take it. And from big families, like we had, just one child remained, or just the mother remained. And  the children all died out. We had quite a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the-- where were your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were still in Budapest-- I was hoping. But my-- my good luck, they were in Budapest. And from there, we-- went to-- to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was again in Belsen-Bergen, a hospital. Which one? I presume they would be called like military barracks. There was like a lot of rooms. And they had like a communal bathroom-- and two floors high, I think, there was just. And across the street, there was like the kitchen. So you had like a-- you went to the kitchen to get your food. I don't remember if we got something like coupons or something to retrieve the food we eat. But I was so weak, I couldn't even go for the food. And we moved in about a dozen girls to that one room. We laid on the floor. We had no other provisions. And one day, my girlfriend is hollering through the window that the ambulance is here. And she said that they should take me to the hospital, since I am not able to take care of myself. And she cannot take care of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went away. They got-- whatever I had done, I was laying out in the sun to try to get warmed. So they took away to that military hospital type of thing. And the doctor came and looked us over. And eventually, we got bunks too.

speaker: interviewee
text: There where I was for a little while, an Hungarian nationals-- military-- they were doing the service duty. Like, they brought us water to get washed in the morning. And they brought the food for us. So there was pretty good there. Only trouble I had-- the sores were very slow on healing from the typhoid-- Fleck typhus. And I was so malnutritious, I weighted probably 50 pounds-- maybe 50 pounds-- 25 kilos. And with that bathroom business, I couldn't put no weight on. I can't do that. Where I put it in, it came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So made a friendship with a girl over there, and she lived in Israel for a number of years. We decided we are step-sisters. She comes from Budapest too. So I don't remember how we agreed her father married my mother or my father married his-- her mother. It doesn't matter-- two different names. And we were a year apart. So that was fine. And we did hold together for quite sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody needed somebody. It was like a mental support. She had thrombosis . So she barely could walk. So actually, I didn't make a big friendship that for my benefit-- account of she couldn't help me too much. But that was a good feeling to have somebody. And from there, we signed up for Sweden. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Took us to Lubeck. And they gave us a shower there and then put us on the boat. And two days later, we were in Sweden on beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what-- at what point did you contact your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I contacted my parents in that military hospital. That was my birthday. And they should make us feel more ease. They gave us paper to write, and pen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote to my parents with the paper and pen that I just came. I was just across the way in town to the dentist, and I had myself a teeth pulled, which one was bothering from the time before we left Hungary. So I had my teeth pulled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was writing to my parents about that and that what I gonna do when I gonna get home, that I gonna eat-- not realizing they had nothing to eat neither-- then just to get my strength back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a-- the letter got sent off. I don't know if-- which garbage can they went, account of there was no communication between the countries yet. And I heard from them 90-- 5-- '45-- New Year's Eve. I had a message from the curator that I have a-- a letter. She want to see me. So this came through the Red Cross-- a letter from my mother. In Europe, they had lists of survivors. So they found me on the list. So they saw my mother after. She had me going crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wrote this letter, which one eventually ended up. The letter was a couple of months old. But she was complaining that her only child she lost and whatever else went with it-- her parents, and her brothers, and all that. There was one single brother remained, that she knew about it, account of he was hiding on the roofs. He came back from his labor camp. And he was hiding on the roofs. So this how he survived. So a year-- a 1/2 a year after the war, I heard about my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did you get reunited with them?

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? You got to Sweden, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got to Sweden with this girlfriend of mine, Alice. And we held together. We ended up in Malmo.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were sick-- both of us. I was actually malnutrition-- nothing-- and the dysentery-- nothing else which one you would call physically sick. I-- I weathered very well, should we say? I was lucky. I come from a-- a very strong stock from both sides-- from my mother's side, and it looked like my father was a very strong man too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Sweden June 28, 19-- '45 and ended up in Malmo. But my sores were so vicious looking, the nurses went off. The doctor had to wash me. They gave you-- you see-- they washed you like a-- scrubbed you. And then they put you under a shower-- like to have you disinfected. We had so many different germs, they tried to eliminate as much as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So ended up in -- in a convalescent home like in Malmo, in a hospital. The hospital was a high school. And I was forbidden to use the facilities. I had a private little closet with a-- with a bucket-- was prepared with a disinfectant and all. That's all what I could use. So you were put in quarantine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my private quarantine. So I couldn't use the bathrooms. I had to go always on that bucket and put some more disinfect on top of it each time I used it, which happened five, six times a day. And from after three weeks, we went to [NON-ENGLISH]-- the whole bunch of girls. And in [NON-ENGLISH], I was getting a little bit stronger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the hospital, we were again in a school. So I was one of the nurses, like the ones which one were like bedridden. I give them water-- bring them water to get washed and to try to sweep up the room every morning. You know, this one was a little bit difficult to sweep up the room. But I'm-- I tried-- managed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But slowly, I was getting a little bit stronger. And after a while, they put us in a convalescent home. The convalescent home was a tourist home. And from there, we were getting jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there where I met my husband-- the only fellow hanging around in the neighborhood. And I worked in a weavery rolling up yarn. They were making like upholstery covers. It was a big factory, but they were very nice, the people there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's something like through the Red Cross we got those jobs. And the curator, she was a foreigner herself. She was an Englishwoman. And her husband was a Dane. So she knew the feeling of being put out of your country. They were political, from Denmark. So her husband worked in the factory too. And-- and we got along fine. We made a very little bit of money. And then one day, we decided we should go to a bigger city to have a better future. Apartments was out of the question. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after you were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. Well, we were courting each other-- with my husband-- for over a year. And we moved to a bigger city called Boras. We got married in Boras when we were in Boras, but we knew each other for quite some time. And if a few-- I had another-- a lot of friends there, which one we had known before-- in between. And-- and we were like in the same situation. We became, you know, like attached to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were all friends. They were Hungarian, Polish, Romanians-- what kind of a girls, it doesn't matter. We were all friends. We are friends with some of the people till today-- after all those years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had very hard times in Sweden too-- financially. The lady who kept us in the tourist home-- she took like 28 kroner a week for food and board. She gave us breakfast. She gave us a sandwich alone for lunch. And she gave us supper. But we were so starved. Her food wasn't sufficient.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever few dollars we-- few krona we had, we all spend it on more food. And clothing, we had just more or less 1 and a 1/2 changes of whatever the Red Cross gave us. Sweden has expensive clothing, so there was very difficult to come by anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we move to Boras. It's a bigger industrial city. And the apartment and the work goes together. If you work for a factory like [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH], they provide you with the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was you and your husband who moved together-- to Boras?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved-- we moved to the same place, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And two other girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and then this was what date? You got married in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married November '46-- November 10th. And we moved there September there-- September-- the beginning of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We made off better financially, naturally. And I changed jobs in between. From the weavery, I went and worked in a manufacturing. I usually made the hems on men's slacks. It was a men's clothing factory. And I made a lot of different things. This was my first experience with an electric sewing machine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you spoken to your parents at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got the letter '45, New Year's Eve. I think that was New Year's Day when I got the letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- but you didn't want to go and see it. You couldn't go and see them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't afford it financially-- even to consider it. I did send them a few packages, which one drained me completely financially. They had nothing in Hungary after the war neither. Papa wanted shoes-- you know, like that would be such an easy thing like somebody would ask you to send me, not considering that I have to provide for myself. It's very difficult to save from that little one what I made when I had nothing myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right Right. So they-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --they so-- so you got married. And then what happened after you got married in 1946?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got married, we had my son before the year. And we were very happy with him. In the beginning, not-- I didn't feel good. But then we were very happy with the little guy. And I worked in between a little bit. I worked till about five weeks before he was born. And we lived nicely quietly-- should we say-- a couple of mishaps here and there, like he got pneumonia, or things like that, which one--

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you made application to go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. We were married a week when we got the first letter from his brother-- that brother, don't get married. And I felt terrible. Man never met me, and he already have a bad opinion of me. But it would have been easier to emigrate to the United States if you haven't got a wife. And then on top of it, I had a baby yet too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had another one how soon afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years and a week. I had a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was after that that you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- she was 20 months old. We came in '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: March. We left Sweden March the 20th-- or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got here, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got here, there is a Jewish saying. We were on [NON-ENGLISH]-- [LAUGHS] --if you know what that means. We arrived here, and nobody waited for us to begin with. We came with the Gripsholm, a Swedish a luxury liner. The Joint paid us some of the car fare. We had paid like a child's carfare-- a few hundred Swedish kroner we paid. They asked us if we could contribute something. So we paid like a child's fare. We had a private cabin. And we had a marvelous trip. We were sea sick from time to time. But beside that, we had a wonderful time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came over here. And we are getting off the boat. And nobody's waiting for us. I says, Edward, what are we going to do with this big country?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter decided she had to make number two. And she was housebroken already. I said, do it in the diaper. So the poor kid was smelling like unbelievable-- but couldn't help it. And then all of a sudden, till we cleared with the papers, it was already lunchtime. The kids were complaining they are hungry. Breakfast was very long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had some American money. So I went over to a peddler, and I bought them some cake and milk or coffee. I don't even remember-- something like that-- just to keep him quiet. And he stood in line with the papers. All of a sudden, a cousin shows up-- a cousin which one he knew, but he had never met.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a single fellow. And he came from Germany. And he didn't work. So he came to meet us. So we got to Brooklyn to his brother's house. And here we arrive a few days before Passover. Sweden is a very clean country. And here we come to East New York, which one is not the nicest neighborhood, even in those days-- garbage cans piled that high-- front of their window.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we come with the taxi-- one taxi driver thew us out account on of the kid was smelling so bad. And another one took us in. And we arriving there. I don't remember who paid for the car fare, but we have three crates, so needed a private crater to storages for us. How much we understood already-- what he had in mind-- I want to take my luggage with me. But this was in the hold. So we were arriving there, and my sister-in-law comes out to greet us, holding her mouth. She was pregnant. And she lost her front teeth. I don't know how many. And she was very embarrassed. But for a pregnant woman, they wouldn't make the teeth. So she was very embarrassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a few weeks before they had lost a business. So they were financially strapped and health-wise strapped. So they weren't very happy. And here we are just before Passover with two kids. And we felt very confined. They had a one-bedroom apartment. And we slept in the living room on a cot. And my little girl got sick. And she was crying. And the kids couldn't get along account of my son spoke Swedish, and my niece speak Yiddish. So I mean, they haven't got a common language. They were the common age. And that's all what they had. And I was crying more than I was eating. And then one day, we went to his aunt. And his aunt have a-- had a beautiful two-family homes. And they were spacious and roomy. And we got a whole room for ourselves-- and let me cook in the kitchen-- not that I'm a crazy cook. But she went to work, not realizing they are poor people too-- just they were lucky enough-- sold one house and bought another one. And we stood there for a few days and then went back. And we got this apartment. Under the table, my husband paid $250 for the filth and cockroach-infested apartment. And we actually belonged to the Joint, not to the HIAS, like my husband says. And the Joint from Park Row took care of us-- how much they took care of us-- gave us like $22 a week for food, for one or two weeks. And they told us we should use up the money what we have on the passport, the $600.

speaker: interviewer
text: So try to tell him that we had to pay the money for under the table, and for paint, and all that. They weren't interested. And they gave us $55 to buy two room of furniture. How could you buy two rooms of furniture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place they sent us to buy the furniture, we couldn't find. There was over a railroad tracks a-- a junk dealer. We couldn't find a house number. So we never found the junk dealer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we bought a kitchen set. And a cousin gave us a crib. And we bought a bed for my son. This is what happened to the $55.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he couldn't get a job. He tried. And I ended up, after five weeks-- my brother-in-law got me a job sewing labels in men's shirts. But they just paid $35 a week. And you had to work like a horse. And I worked there for a few weeks. And I says that I need more money. I explained to the boss that I need more money. I said, I had to pay carfare to go to New York. Somewhere on 35th Street was the store. It wasn't a store-- like, above the store. And I need more money. I used up money for carfare-- $0.15 cost a day. And my husband was getting little private jobs here and there. For a 1/2 a day, make buttonhole. For a day, he'd make a buttonhole. So we had a very hard beginning. But thank God, we brought up the kids and got independent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever spoken about this to your-- with your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, each time something came to be mentioned, I didn't think they were interested. My son says that not that they weren't interested-- we were busy people trying to make a living, trying to give the things what we didn't have. We didn't have really the time, I presume, to sit down to have like a discussion over it. This is not a spur of the moment that you just want to say something about it-- or you say a lot, or you don't say nothing. So I presume we don't do nothing. They knew that we are survivors. They know that grandpa have a-- even the grandchildren know the numbers on his arm, and then all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when did your parents come?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived with me for three years, which one was the biggest mistake. It's a different lifes time, different attitude, different people. They came from a communist regime to begin with. And my father had no knowledge whatsoever of being Jewish. So he completely ignored everything. And they couldn't get along in that respect-- with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father bought in a-- a tray for food. And he wanted to put it in the Frigidaire. And he was hollering that I don't want it in my Frigidaire. I said, so what should I do between the two of you? So I was a-- a bit-- a bit in a fix. Mama was running the household for a while. And my father thought that he pays the mortgage on the house since he paid me $55 once a month. This was for food and for the rent-- he contributed. He thought he pays the whole mortgage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And bad feelings came of it account of we didn't agree on a lot of things. So one day, he got mad at me account of some keys, and moved out.
